heroDescription: Fast-Casual QSR • 256 Restaurants • A$468M Revenue • IPO June 2024
heroCompanyDescription: Guzman y Gomez (ASX: GYG) is an Australian fast-casual restaurant chain specialising in Mexican-inspired food with a clean-ingredient positioning. Founded in 2006 by Steven Marks, GYG listed on the ASX in June 2024 at A$22.00 per share, peaking at A$45.76 within eight months before correcting to A$17.53. The company operates 256 restaurants (FY25) across Australia, Singapore, Japan, and the United States, with 108 approved sites in the pipeline. Restaurant-level economics are strong (22% margins, 48-53% franchisee ROI), but comparable sales have decelerated from 15% (FY23) to 4-5% (H1 FY26). US operations are loss-making (A$13.2M EBITDA loss FY25) with the parent entity having written off its entire A$28.2M US investment.

skew.rationale: 72% combined weight on downside scenarios (N2 Decelerating Growth at 46% + N3 US Capital Destruction at 21% + N4 Franchise Stress at 5%). Evidence-weighted price A$13.59 vs market A$17.53. The market overweights the compounder narrative (N1) by 19 percentage points relative to the evidence. Three consecutive negative filing-day reactions confirm the pattern.

verdict.text: GYG is trading at A$17.53, 29% above the evidence-weighted price of approximately A$13.59. The market assigns 47% probability to the Australian Compounder narrative; the evidence supports 28%. Three consecutive half-year results have produced escalating negative filing-day reactions (−9.2%, −13.9%, −17.6%), and comparable sales have decelerated from 15% (FY23) to 4–5% (H1 FY26). Unit economics remain strong: drive-thru AUV of A$6.9M, restaurant-level margins of 22%, and franchisee ROI of 48–53%. The tension is real: excellent unit economics inside a decelerating growth trajectory. US operations are actively destroying value (A$13.2M EBITDA loss FY25, losses doubling annually), and insider control (44%) prevents course correction. The H1 FY26 results will resolve more uncertainty in a single day than months of analysis can provide. WAIT for results.

featuredRationale: Evidence-weighted price 23% below market. Three consecutive negative filing-day reactions. Comps decelerating from 15% to 4%. Unit economics strong but growth trajectory weakening. US losses doubling annually. Verdict: WAIT for H1 FY26 results.

paragraphs: GYG listed at A$22 in June 2024 and peaked at A$45.76 within eight months, pricing the company at over 90 times trailing EBITDA. The market was paying for a rare Australian fast-casual compounder with institutional-grade unit economics: drive-thru AUV of A$6.9M, restaurant-level margins of 22%, franchisee ROI above 48%, and a repeat franchisee rate of 64%. That multiple has since compressed 74% to 23 times trailing EBITDA.

The compression reflects three converging forces. First, comparable sales have decelerated from 15% (FY23) to 8.1% (FY24) to 4–5% (H1 FY26). McDonald's overtook GYG on Australian same-store sales growth in Q4 2025. Second, US operations are actively destroying value: A$13.2M EBITDA loss in FY25 with losses doubling annually and the parent entity writing off its entire A$28.2M US investment to zero. Third, governance structure (44% insider/aligned control, LTI with zero performance conditions, no board majority independence) prevents external accountability on capital allocation.

We frame the investment case around four competing scenarios. The market assigns 47% probability to the compounder narrative (N1). The evidence supports only 28%. The dominant scenario by evidence weight is N2: Decelerating Growth Operator at 46%, reflecting the mathematical reality that comparable sales deceleration from 15% to 4% implies a mature franchise operator, not a hyper-growth compounder. The evidence-weighted price is A$13.59, approximately 23% below the current market price of A$17.53.

The H1 FY26 results represent the single most important discriminating event. Australian comparable sales growth for Q3 and Q4 will bifurcate the probability distribution. Above 6%: the deceleration was cyclical and N1 regains credibility. Between 3% and 6%: N2 is confirmed. Below 3%: pure inflation pass-through, and the stock faces a further 30–40% downside. The structural position is WAIT. Results resolve more uncertainty in one day than months of analysis can provide.

identity.overview: Guzman y Gomez (ASX: GYG) is an Australian fast-casual restaurant chain founded in 2006 by Steven Marks. The company listed on the ASX in June 2024 at A$22.00 per share, with TDM Growth Partners (20.6%), Steven Marks (9.6%), Barrenjoey Private Capital (8.1%), and Guy Russo (6.0%) forming a 44.3% insider/aligned bloc. GYG operates 256 restaurants across Australia, Singapore, Japan, and the United States, with a 108-site approved pipeline. The Australian franchise system generates industry-leading unit economics (22% restaurant-level margins, 48-53% franchisee ROI, 64% repeat franchisee rate). US operations are loss-making at negative 57.5% corporate restaurant margins. The A$29.6B Australian QSR market grows at 2.6% annually, meaning GYG's growth comes from share theft, not market expansion.

hypotheses.title: N1: Australian Compounder Temporarily Mispriced
hypotheses.statusText: Overpriced by market: 47% implied vs 28% evidence
hypotheses.description: The market conflates US failure, escrow supply, and cyclical deceleration with core domestic business health. Australian unit economics are widening: drive-thru AUV grew from A$3.0M (FY20) to A$6.9M (H1 FY26), restaurant-level margins sit at 22% (top of industry range), and franchisee ROI exceeds 48%. If comparable sales stabilise above 4% and the drive-thru pipeline converts at 23+ openings per year, GYG can compound at 15–20% revenue growth for the next three to five years.
requires: Australian comparable sales stabilise above 4% and demonstrate re-acceleration toward 6%+
Drive-thru pipeline converts at 23+ openings per year from 108 approved sites
US losses do not escalate beyond current levels (15-restaurant cap holds)
Post-escrow selling clears within 6 months (TDM sell-down normalises)
No competitive convergence from McDonald's or KFC on clean-food positioning
supporting: Drive-thru AUV growth from A$3.0M to A$6.9M: 130% increase in five years, demonstrating genuine consumer demand
Restaurant-level margins at 22%: top of the 10–22% industry range, proving the unit economic model works
Franchisee median ROI of 48–53%: industry-leading, reflected in 64% repeat franchisee rate for recent openings
A$281.7M cash, zero debt: fortress balance sheet eliminates solvency risk entirely
contradicting: Comps deceleration from 15% to 4%: three consecutive periods of declining growth, pattern unbroken
McDonald's overtook GYG on comps in Q4 2025: competitive positioning weakening at precisely the wrong time
Three consecutive negative filing-day reactions (−9.2%, −13.9%, −17.6%): escalating severity, no base formation
Down-day volume 1.87× up-day volume: capitulation indicator, not accumulation
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N1 is the only scenario where the IPO promise of a generational Australian compounder remains intact. It requires the comparable sales deceleration to prove cyclical (driven by escrow supply overhang and post-IPO normalisation) rather than structural. The H1 FY26 comparable sales figure is the binary test.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: McDonald's competitive response. If the largest QSR operator in Australia moves toward cleaner ingredients and outspends GYG on marketing in a cost-of-living environment, GYG's pricing power erodes. Q4 2025 showed the first evidence of this: McDonald's comps exceeded GYG's for the first time.

hypotheses.title: N2: Decelerating Growth Operator
hypotheses.description: Growth is normalising from the hyper-growth IPO phase. The IPO multiple was scarcity-driven mispricing: the first listed fast-casual QSR operator on the ASX attracted momentum capital that has since unwound. Comparable sales continue decelerating toward 2–3%, McDonald's and KFC adapt toward cleaner ingredients, and the market consensus settles on a mature franchise operator trading at 15× EBITDA rather than 90×.
requires: Comps continue decelerating toward 2–3%, entering pure inflation pass-through territory
McDonald's and KFC adapt toward cleaner ingredients, narrowing GYG's differentiation
Market consensus settles on mature franchise operator at 12–18× EBITDA
US losses persist as consolidated drag without exit criteria being met
supporting: Comps trajectory: 15% (FY23) to 8.1% (FY24) to 4–5% (H1 FY26): consistent deceleration across three periods
EBIT margins expand only above 15–20% revenue growth: current 4–5% comps imply margin compression
Negative filing-day pattern escalating: −9.2%, −13.9%, −17.6% across three consecutive results
Share-based compensation consumed 62% of FY25 statutory net profit: dilution is real
EV/EBITDA compressed from 90× to 23×: still expensive for projected 10% revenue growth
contradicting: Unit economics are widening, not narrowing: AUV, margins, and franchisee ROI all improving
108-site pipeline provides 4–5 years of store-level growth: revenue growth can persist even if comps slow
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N2 is the scenario where excellent unit economics coexist with a decelerating growth rate. The franchise works at the restaurant level, but the aggregate growth trajectory that justified the IPO multiple is fading. This is the most common outcome for fast-casual QSR operators post-IPO.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: Comps re-acceleration. If H1 FY26 comps come in above 6%, the deceleration thesis weakens materially. Two consecutive quarters above 8% would rule N2 out entirely and shift weight back to N1.

hypotheses.title: N3: US Capital Destruction Accelerates
hypotheses.description: The US expansion is actively destroying value. Corporate restaurant margins of negative 57.5%, cumulative unrecognised tax losses of A$29.2M, and the parent entity writing off its entire A$28.2M US investment to zero. Losses are doubling annually. The governance structure (44% insider control) prevents the board from forcing a course correction. The 15-restaurant cap is management-imposed and non-binding.
requires: US losses continue doubling annually (FY25 A$13.2M EBITDA loss; implies FY26 A$20M+)
Management lifts US cap without binding exit criteria or quantified milestones
Governance structure prevents effective oversight on US capital allocation
Potential US impairment of A$10–20M representing 70–140% of FY25 statutory NPAT
supporting: FY25 US EBITDA loss A$13.2M: doubling from prior year
Corporate restaurant margins −57.5%: deeply negative unit economics
Parent entity wrote off entire A$28.2M US investment to zero: management's own assessment
Cumulative unrecognised US tax losses A$29.2M: growing annual drag
US "proof of concept" undefined: no quantified milestones for expansion or exit
contradicting: 15-restaurant cap limits absolute dollar exposure: US losses capped at approximately A$20–25M annually
A$281.7M cash buffer: US losses are a drag, not an existential threat
US losses represent ~3% of total revenue: material but not dominant
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N3 is the scenario where the US expansion becomes the defining characteristic of management quality. The domestic franchise system proves that the team can build and operate restaurants. The US expansion proves they cannot allocate capital rationally when governance structures fail to constrain them.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: US restaurant-level margin turning positive. Even one quarter of positive margins at the US restaurant level would weaken N3 significantly by demonstrating the unit economic model can work outside Australia.

hypotheses.title: N4: Franchise System Stress
hypotheses.description: Headline franchise metrics mask emerging system stress. Franchisees on royalty relief have increased from 4 to 6. Median franchise ROI, while strong at 48–53%, could decline as cost-of-living pressures hit labour costs and new restaurants cannibalise existing ones. If royalty relief escalates beyond 10 stores and the franchise flywheel runs in reverse, the system deteriorates.
requires: Franchisees on royalty relief escalate beyond 10 (currently 6, up from 4)
Forced reacquisition pace increases as franchisees become unprofitable
Customer-facing quality deteriorates visibly affecting brand perception
supporting: Royalty relief count increased from 4 to 6: direction is wrong
Expected credit losses trajectory worsening: A$603K on A$11.5M receivables, rising
Supply chain concentration (top 5 suppliers 60% of purchases): limited negotiating flexibility on proprietary ingredients
contradicting: Franchisee ROI at 48–53% is industry-leading: system is healthy by any objective measure
64% repeat franchisee rate: existing operators are expanding, not contracting
Restaurant-level margins at 22%: top of the 10–22% industry range
Head lease default exposure only ~A$16.5M at 10% assumption: manageable
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N4 monitors the franchise system health indicators that precede visible deterioration. By the time median franchisee ROI falls below 40% or royalty relief exceeds 10 stores, the system is already in decline. This is the early-warning scenario.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The counter-evidence is overwhelming. Franchisee ROI at 48–53% with a 64% repeat rate makes system stress implausible in the near term. N4 exists as a tail risk to monitor, not a central scenario.

narrative.theNarrative: GYG listed at A$22 in June 2024 and peaked at A$45.76 within eight months, pricing the company at over 90× trailing EV/EBITDA. The market was paying for scarcity: the first listed fast-casual QSR operator on the ASX with institutional-grade unit economics. That premium has since compressed 74% to 23× trailing EBITDA. Three forces are driving the correction. Comparable sales have decelerated from 15% to 4–5%. US operations are actively destroying capital (A$13.2M EBITDA loss, losses doubling annually). Three consecutive half-year results have produced escalating negative filing-day reactions. The central tension: unit economics are excellent and widening (drive-thru AUV up 130% in five years, restaurant margins at 22%, franchisee ROI above 48%) while the aggregate growth rate that justified the IPO multiple is fading. The H1 FY26 comparable sales figure bifurcates the investment case.

priceImplication.label: Embedded Assumptions at A$17.53
priceImplication.content: At A$17.53, the market embeds roughly 47% probability on the compounder narrative (N1) and 53% on downside scenarios (N2+N3+N4). The evidence assigns 28% to N1 and 72% to downside. The disagreement centres on whether the comparable sales deceleration is cyclical (post-IPO normalisation, escrow supply overhang) or structural (mature franchise growth rate converging toward the 2.6% industry average). If comps re-accelerate above 6% in H1 FY26, N1 gains 8–12 percentage points and the evidence-weighted price rises to A$15–17. If comps fall below 3%, N2 rises to 52%+ and the evidence-weighted price falls to A$11–12, implying a further 30–40% downside.

narrative.evidenceCheck: The evidence base is bifurcated. Unit economic metrics are uniformly strong: restaurant-level margins (22%), franchisee ROI (48–53%), AUV growth (A$3.0M to A$6.9M), G&A compression (6.7% to 5.8% of network sales), and repeat franchisee rate (64%). These are independently verifiable from statutory filings and have been consistent across multiple reporting periods. Against this, growth indicators are uniformly weakening: comparable sales decelerating, EBIT margins compressing below 15% revenue growth, filing-day reactions escalating negatively, and McDonald's overtaking on comps. The strongest evidence domains are statutory financials and objective competitive data; the weakest are delivery channel economics (undisclosed), US operations transparency (minimal), and governance accountability (absent).
narrative.narrativeStability: LOW. The H1 FY26 results represent a high-magnitude discriminating event. Comparable sales above 6% would shift N1 by 8–12 percentage points. Below 3% would shift N2 by 6–10 percentage points. A single day's data could move the evidence-weighted price by 15–25%. The investment case is in a holding pattern until results are released.

qualityOfEarnings.inflectionDependency: FY25 delivered the first positive operating cash flow (A$57.3M vs negative A$9.7M in FY24) and the first positive half of free cash flow (A$1.1M in H2 FY25). These inflection points are genuine but fragile. At 4–5% comparable sales growth, operating leverage works in reverse: revenue growth below 15% implies margin compression, not expansion. The trajectory depends entirely on whether comps stabilise or continue decelerating.
qualityOfEarnings.threePhaseArc: Phase one: the IPO growth story. Revenue compounding at 28–50% annually, multiple expanding to 90× EBITDA, market pricing five years of hyper-growth. Phase two: reality check. Three consecutive negative filing-day reactions, comps decelerating from 15% to 4%, US losses doubling, escrow supply hitting the register. Phase three: mature operator re-rating. If comps settle in the 3–6% range, GYG re-rates from growth stock to mature franchise operator at 12–18× EBITDA. The question is whether phase two is transitional or terminal.
qualityOfEarnings.roeFlatterers: Share-based compensation consumed 62% of FY25 statutory net profit (A$14.4M). This dilution is real and must be considered alongside headline earnings. The Sloan Accrual Ratio of −0.05 is favourable (low accruals, high cash conversion). The Beneish M-Score of −2.55 indicates low manipulation risk. OCF/NI of 3.96× confirms cash-backed earnings. The earnings quality is high; the earnings quantity is the problem.
qualityOfEarnings.balanceSheetFragility: The balance sheet is a fortress: A$281.7M cash, zero interest-bearing debt, net cash per share of approximately A$2.73. Solvency risk is absent under any plausible scenario. This eliminates the balance sheet as a source of downside surprise. The risk is entirely income-statement-driven (growth deceleration, margin compression, US losses) rather than balance-sheet-driven.

evidence.intro: Ten evidence domains assessed across the epistemic hierarchy, updated for February 2026. Evidence ranked: Audited Statutory > Objective Data > Independent Sources > Consensus > Motivated > Noise.

cards.title: 1. Corporate Communications
cards.finding: Management frames deceleration as normalisation, not structural decline. FY26 guidance: 32 gross restaurant openings (23 drive-thru, 9 strip), segment EBITDA margin 6.0–6.2% of network sales. Management targets royalty rate above 10% within five years of IPO (currently 8.6%) and G&A below 5% of network sales (currently 5.8%). US positioning remains as "proof of concept" with no defined exit criteria or quantified milestones. Dividend programme initiated: maiden 12.6 cps paid September 2025. A$100M buyback announced at board discretion.
cards.tension: Management guidance on Australian operations has been reliable (guided 26 openings in FY24, delivered; guided 30 in FY25, revised to 31, delivered). US guidance is systematically vague with no measurable milestones. The framing of US operations as "proof of concept" allows indefinite capital destruction without triggering governance accountability.
cards.source: GYG FY25 Results Presentation; ASX announcements; Investor presentations

cards.title: 2. Regulatory Filings & Financial Statements
cards.finding: Cash flow inflection confirmed; growth deceleration confirmed; US write-off confirmed. FY25 revenue A$468M (+28%). OCF A$57.3M (inflection from negative A$9.7M). FCF still negative (A$7.2M) but H2 FY25 positive (A$1.1M). Gross margin stable at 75.7%. EBIT margin 5.4%. ROE 3.9%. Net income A$14.4M. Parent entity wrote off entire A$28.2M US investment to zero. US EBITDA loss A$13.2M. Franchisees on royalty relief increased from 4 to 6.
cards.tension: The statutory filings confirm both the bull case (unit economics work, cash flow inflecting, balance sheet strong) and the bear case (growth decelerating, US destroying value, governance metrics weak). The evidence does not resolve the tension; it sharpens it.
cards.source: GYG FY25 Annual Report; Appendix 4E; Parent entity financials

cards.title: 3. Broker & Consensus Estimates
cards.finding: Post-IPO consensus has been systematically overoptimistic. Broker targets clustered around A$35–40 at IPO. Three consecutive negative filing-day reactions suggest the sell-side has not kept pace with the deceleration. No evidence of coordinated downgrade cycle, but the escalating severity of filing-day reactions (−9.2%, −13.9%, −17.6%) implies the market is pricing in disappointment faster than analysts are revising models.
cards.tension: Sell-side coverage was initiated at IPO with growth assumptions that have not materialised. The pattern of escalating negative filing-day reactions is diagnostic: institutional investors are selling into results faster each time. This pattern typically precedes a major consensus revision cycle.
cards.source: Bloomberg consensus; ASX market data; Filing-day return analysis

cards.title: 4. Competitor & Industry Data
cards.finding: Australian QSR is a 2.6% growth market; GYG wins through share theft, not market expansion. The A$29.6B market supports over 5,000 QSR outlets with 75%+ of Australians within 3km of both Subway and McDonald's. GYG's growth requires taking customers from incumbents. McDonald's overtook GYG on Australian same-store sales growth in Q4 2025, the first time this has happened since GYG's listing. McDonald's promotional intensity in cost-of-living environments is the primary competitive threat to GYG's pricing power.
cards.tension: GYG's clean-food differentiation is genuine (100% proprietary ingredients, no freezers in stores). Whether this positioning sustains a premium when the largest QSR operator adapts its marketing toward health-conscious consumers is the competitive question. GYG's delivery exposure (26.6% of sales) adds margin vulnerability through platform commissions.
cards.source: IBISWorld; ABS retail data; Company filings; Industry analysis

cards.title: 5. Economic & Regulatory Data
cards.finding: Cost-of-living pressures compress discretionary spending and increase labour costs simultaneously. Australian minimum wage increases (4.6% July 2024, 3.75% July 2025) directly pressure QSR labour costs (25–35% of revenue). Hospitality labour shortages persist. Consumer confidence remains below long-run average. Delivery platform commissions (25–35% of order value) compress margins on GYG's 26.6% delivery channel without full cost disclosure.
cards.tension: Economic conditions create a scissors effect: input costs rise (labour, ingredients) while consumer willingness to pay a premium weakens. GYG's average transaction value of A$21.27 is 14% above QSR average, making it more exposed to trade-down than mass-market competitors.
cards.source: ABS; Fair Work Commission; RBA; Industry reports

cards.title: 6. Price Action & Market Behaviour
cards.finding: Post-IPO multiple compression of 74% with no base formation. GYG peaked at A$45.76 (90.4× EV/EBITDA) within eight months of IPO and has since declined to A$17.53 (23.1×). Three consecutive negative filing-day reactions with escalating severity (−9.2%, −13.9%, −17.6%). Down-day volume runs at 1.87× up-day volume. No evidence of institutional accumulation at any price level. The stock is now trading below its A$22 IPO price.
cards.tension: The relentless decline could reflect either the market correctly pricing a decelerating growth story or an overcorrection creating opportunity. The escalating filing-day pattern with no base formation favours the former interpretation. The absence of value-buyer accumulation despite fortress balance sheet and strong unit economics is notable.
cards.source: ASX market data; GYG price and volume history; Filing-day return analysis

cards.title: 7. Academic & Structural Research
cards.finding: IPO multiple compression is the norm, not the exception, for fast-casual QSR listings. Academic literature on restaurant IPOs shows that hyper-growth multiples rarely sustain beyond 18–24 months as the market reprices toward mature operator multiples. Franchise system health research identifies three leading indicators of stress: declining franchisee ROI, increasing royalty relief, and rising reacquisition rates. GYG currently triggers one of three (royalty relief trending up).
cards.tension: The academic base rate for fast-casual IPO multiple compression supports N2. The franchise health literature provides N4 monitoring indicators but current readings (ROI 48–53%, relief at 6 stores) remain well within healthy ranges.
cards.source: Restaurant IPO performance literature; Franchise system health research

cards.title: 8. Media & Public Commentary
cards.finding: Retail sentiment has turned negative; institutional coverage is limited post-IPO. Social media and retail investor commentary has shifted from IPO enthusiasm to disappointment. Coverage of the US expansion losses is increasing. The narrative has shifted from "the next Chipotle" to "overpriced IPO unwind". This sentiment shift, while lagging, reflects the same growth deceleration visible in the data.
cards.tension: Retail sentiment is a coincident indicator, not a leading one. The shift from bullish to bearish reflects price action rather than driving it. Low analytical value.

cards.title: 9. Leadership & Governance Assessment
cards.finding: Insider control (44%) with weak accountability structures. Four aligned holders control 44.3% of votes: TDM Growth Partners (20.6%), Steven Marks (9.6%), Barrenjoey Private Capital (8.1%), Guy Russo (6.0%). Board independence is 43% (lacks majority). Remuneration and Nomination Committee has 2/3 non-independent members. LTI vests on time alone with zero performance conditions. No clawback provisions, succession plan, or board effectiveness review disclosed. CEO limited-recourse loan facilities of A$31.1M.
cards.tension: The governance structure allows management to pursue value-destructive strategies (US expansion) without effective accountability. The domestic franchise operations demonstrate operational competence, making the governance risk selective rather than pervasive. The question is whether the capital allocation failures (US) eventually contaminate the operational strengths (Australia).
cards.source: GYG Annual Report; Corporate governance statement; ASX substantial holder notices

cards.title: 10. Ownership & Capital Flows
cards.finding: Insider selling and escrow overhang dominate capital flows. TDM Growth Partners reduced its managed stake from 36.1% at IPO to 20.6% by August 2025, though the reduction reflects client mandate transfers rather than a fundamental view change (TDM's beneficial holding is only 105,000 shares). Outstanding shares grew from ~91.5M at IPO to 103M. Two escrow releases: 25% of pre-IPO shares in March 2025 and 75% in August 2025. The supply overhang has depressed the stock but should normalise within 6–12 months.
cards.tension: The escrow sell-down creates a one-time supply headwind that N1 proponents can cite as a temporary distortion. If the stock fails to recover once escrow selling clears, the deceleration thesis (N2) gains credibility. The timing of escrow resolution coincides with H1 FY26 results, making it difficult to disentangle supply effects from fundamental re-pricing.
cards.source: ASX substantial holder notices; Share registry data; Escrow timetable

discriminators.intro: Four data points carry high diagnosticity for discriminating between GYG's competing hypotheses. The H1 FY26 results represent the highest-magnitude single event.

rows.evidence: Australian comparable sales: H1 FY26 quarterly data. The single most important metric. Above 6%: deceleration proves cyclical, N1 gains 8–12 percentage points. 3–6%: N2 confirmed, no shift exceeds 5pp. Below 3%: pure inflation territory, N2 rises to 52%+, evidence-weighted price falls to A$11–12.
rows.currentReading: 4.0–4.8% in H1 FY26: in the confirmation zone for N2

rows.evidence: US EBITDA loss trajectory: H1 FY26. If US losses continue doubling (H1 FY26 loss exceeds A$8M, annualising above A$16M), N3 gains 5–8 percentage points. If US restaurant-level margins turn positive in any quarter, N3 weakens materially.
rows.discriminatesBetween: N3 (US Capital Destruction) vs N2 (manageable drag)
rows.currentReading: A$13.2M FY25 EBITDA loss: doubling from prior year

rows.evidence: Franchisee health indicators: royalty relief count and median ROI. Royalty relief above 10 stores or median ROI below 40% would trigger a significant shift toward N4. Current readings (6 stores on relief, ROI 48–53%) remain healthy but the trajectory is negative.
rows.currentReading: 6 stores on relief (up from 4); ROI 48–53%: healthy but monitoring

rows.evidence: Filing-day reaction: H1 FY26. A positive filing-day reaction would break the escalating negative pattern (−9.2%, −13.9%, −17.6%) and signal a potential regime change. A fourth consecutive negative reaction confirms the market is repricing GYG from growth to mature operator.
rows.currentReading: Three consecutive negatives with escalating severity

discriminators.nonDiscriminating: Retail sentiment (lagging, coincident with price) • Insider selling by TDM (mandate transfer, not fundamental view change) • Share-based compensation levels (structural, not variable) • Buyback activity (at board discretion, price-insensitive signal).

tripwires.intro: Three catalysts that would trigger reassessment of hypothesis weights, with specific thresholds defining positive and negative outcomes.

cards.name: H1 FY26 Results: The Discriminating Event

conditions.if: If Australian comps reach 6%+ with positive filing-day reaction
conditions.then: N1 gains 8–12 percentage points. Evidence-weighted price rises to A$15–17. Skew collapses to BALANCED. Deceleration proves cyclical, not structural. Stock tests A$20–22 (IPO price).

conditions.if: If Australian comps 3–6% with negative filing-day reaction
conditions.then: N2 confirmed at ~46%. No probability shift exceeds 5 percentage points. Stock trades A$14–18 range. Framework rolls to August 2026 confirmation event.
N2 rises to 52%+. Evidence-weighted price falls to A$11–12. Fourth consecutive negative filing-day reaction. Implies 30–40% further downside from current levels.

cards.source: Source: GYG H1 FY26 ASX Announcement

cards.name: FY26 Annual Results

conditions.if: If franchisee relief count declines to 4 or below, and US losses stabilise
conditions.then: N3 loses 3–5 percentage points. N4 monitoring risk recedes. System health confirmed.

conditions.if: If US exit criteria announced with binding milestones
conditions.then: N3 loses 5–8 percentage points as governance accountability improves. Capital allocation discipline restored.

conditions.if: If franchisee relief exceeds 10 or US losses annualise above A$20M
conditions.then: N3 gains 5–8 percentage points. N4 gains 3–5 percentage points. System stress indicators triggering.

cards.source: Source: GYG FY26 Annual Report; ASX Appendix 4E

cards.name: McDonald's vs GYG Competitive Positioning

conditions.if: If GYG comps exceed McDonald's for two consecutive quarters
conditions.then: Competitive positioning restored. N1 gains 3–5 percentage points. Clean-food differentiation holding against promotional intensity.

conditions.if: If McDonald's outperforms GYG on comps for three consecutive quarters
conditions.then: Competitive convergence confirmed. N2 gains 3–5 percentage points. GYG's premium positioning eroding in cost-of-living environment.

cards.source: Source: QSR industry data; Company filings; ABS retail trade

couldntAssess: Delivery channel economics: 26.6% of network sales through high-commission platforms (25–35% of order value). Channel-level margins undisclosed. Delivery revenue likely materially lower-margin than dine-in/drive-thru, potentially eliminating restaurant-level profit on most delivery transactions

US restaurant-level P&L granularity: Aggregate US EBITDA loss (A$13.2M) disclosed but individual store-level economics, customer acquisition costs, and competitive positioning in US markets not available

Franchisee-level financial performance distribution: Median ROI (48–53%) reported but the range, standard deviation, and tail outcomes not disclosed. Six stores on royalty relief but their financial condition unknown

Supply chain contract terms: Top 5 suppliers represent 60% of purchases. Proprietary ingredient sourcing creates high switching costs but contract durations, pricing escalation clauses, and renewal terms undisclosed

Customer cohort retention: Monthly brand reach (~14M people) and transaction age skew (27.7% from 18–24 year-olds) reported, but customer repeat rate, cohort retention, and frequency trends unavailable
gaps.analyticalLimitations: The delivery channel blind spot is the most significant gap. At 26.6% of network sales, undisclosed channel economics could materially affect the profitability trajectory in all scenarios. Platform commissions of 25–35% likely eliminate restaurant-level profit on delivery orders, meaning the 22% restaurant-level margin overstates economic profitability by an unknown amount. Additionally, the US operations lack sufficient disclosure to assess whether the 15-restaurant cap will hold or whether management will expand beyond it under the current governance structure.

crossover.description: 50-day MA crossed below 200-day MA approximately four months after IPO. Both averages have declined continuously since.

trend.structure: Three-phase decline: IPO euphoria unwind (A$45 to A$33, first 3 months), sustained selling (A$33 to A$23, months 3-12), capitulation (A$23 to A$17, months 12-20). No evidence of base formation.

support.method: Round number; approaching psychological support at IPO discount

resistance.method: IPO price; prior support turned resistance

priorSpikes.context: Post-peak profit-taking; initial break below A$35
Death cross week; institutional selling intensifies
Break below IPO price A$22; retail capitulation

inflectionPoints.event: Second escrow release (75%); TDM sell-down visible
Break below IPO price; retail capitulation
New all-time low; third consecutive negative filing-day

conditions: Australian comparable sales re-accelerate above 6% in H1 FY26 (proving deceleration was cyclical)
Drive-thru pipeline converts at 23+ openings per year with AUV above A$6M
US losses stabilise or decline (not escalate further)
Post-escrow selling clears and new institutional buyers emerge
McDonald's does not sustain competitive convergence on clean-food positioning
Australian comparable sales fall below 3%, confirming structural deceleration to inflation pass-through
US EBITDA losses exceed A$8M in H1 FY26 (annualising above A$16M), accelerating capital destruction
McDonald's outperforms GYG on comps for three consecutive quarters, confirming competitive convergence
Franchisees on royalty relief exceed 10, or median ROI falls below 40%
Fourth consecutive negative filing-day reaction confirms market re-rating from growth to mature operator

riskAsymmetry.summary: The asymmetry is unfavourable. The upside scenario (N1 at A$26.34) requires ALL five conditions and carries only 28% weight. The combined downside scenarios (N2 at A$11.77, N3 at A$7.62, N4 at A$3.76) carry 72% weight and require only ONE trigger from any of five independent conditions. The expected loss from downside scenarios exceeds the expected gain from upside. The fortress balance sheet (A$281.7M cash, zero debt) limits tail risk to income-statement impairment rather than existential crisis.
riskAsymmetry.marketDisagreement: The evidence and the market disagree most on the probability that GYG remains a compounder. The market assigns 47% to N1; the evidence supports 28%. This 19 percentage point gap implies approximately A$3.60 per share of mispricing. The market has not yet fully priced the mathematical reality that comparable sales decelerating from 15% to 4% implies a mature franchise operator, not a hyper-growth compounder.

conclusion.opening: The evidence-weighted price is approximately A$13.59. The market is at A$17.53. That is a 23% gap to the downside, driven primarily by the market overweighting the compounder narrative (47% market-implied vs 28% evidence-weighted). The domestic franchise system is genuinely high quality: unit economics are widening, cash flow is inflecting, and the balance sheet is a fortress. But three forces work against the bull case: systematic comparable sales deceleration (15% to 4%), escalating US capital destruction (losses doubling annually), and a governance structure that prevents accountability on capital allocation.

paths.label: Path A
paths.outcome: N1 gains 8–12 percentage points. Evidence-weighted price rises to A$15–17. Skew collapses to BALANCED. The deceleration proves cyclical. Stock tests A$20–22. The market begins pricing GYG as a compounder again rather than a mature operator.

paths.label: Path B
paths.outcome: N2 confirmed at approximately 46%. No probability shift exceeds 5 percentage points. Stock trades A$14–18 range. Framework rolls to August 2026 for confirmation. The re-rating from growth to mature operator continues gradually.

paths.label: Path C
paths.outcome: N2 rises to 52%+. Evidence-weighted price falls to A$11–12. Implies 30–40% further downside from current levels. The compounder narrative is dead. GYG re-rates to 12–15× EBITDA as a mature franchise operator with a US capital destruction problem.

conclusion.closing: The structural position is WAIT. The H1 FY26 results will resolve more uncertainty in a single day than months of forensic analysis can provide. The evidence points toward downside (72% probability below current price), but the discriminating event is imminent. Positioning ahead of a high-magnitude binary event with directional but not robust conviction would be imprudent. After results: re-assess. If Path C materialises, the short case becomes compelling. If Path A materialises, the entire framework requires revision.

hero.embedded_thesis: At A$17.53, the market embeds roughly 47% probability on the compounder narrative and 53% on downside scenarios. The evidence assigns 28% to the compounder and 72% to downside. The disagreement centres on whether comparable sales deceleration from 15% to 4% is cyclical (post-IPO normalisation) or structural (mature franchise growth converging toward industry average).
hero.skew_description: Three downside hypotheses carry 72% combined weight. N2 (Decelerating Growth) at 46% is the modal outcome. N3 (US Capital Destruction) at 21% is accumulating evidence. N4 (Franchise Stress) at 5% is tail risk. N1 (Australian Compounder) at 28% is overpriced by the market (47% implied vs 28% evidence).

position_in_range.note: Scenario-implied prices based on probability-weighted EBITDA multiples. Evidence-weighted fair value: A$13.59.

next_decision_point.metric: Australian comparable sales growth (Q3 and Q4 FY26)
next_decision_point.thresholds: Above 6%: deceleration cyclical, N1 regains credibility. 3-6%: N2 confirmed, stock trades A$14-18. Below 3%: structural deceleration, implies 30-40% further downside.

footer.disclaimer: This report does not constitute personal financial advice. Continuum Intelligence maps financial data against a probability-weighted scenario framework. It describes the direction and magnitude of evidence relative to market pricing. All factual claims are sourced from ASX filings, company results presentations, and publicly available information. This report does not contain buy, sell, or hold recommendations.

industryStructure.title: A$29.6 billion market where incremental growth comes from share theft, not footprint expansion
industryStructure.overview: The Australian QSR market compounded at 2.6% annually over five years. Over 75% of Australians live within 3km of both Subway and McDonald’s. Growth for any individual operator requires taking customers from incumbents, not serving unmet demand.
competitionAxes: Format economics (drive-thru vs strip vs food court)
industryStructure.keyInsight: GYG’s A$6.9M drive-thru AUV and 22% restaurant margins represent top-of-range performance in a market where incumbents are entrenched and incremental growth must come from share theft. The structural question is whether clean-food differentiation sustains a premium in an environment where the largest operator (McDonald’s) is adapting its positioning.

competitivePositioning.title: Domestic position widening at moderate confidence, driven by format economics and franchise system quality
competitivePositioning.overview: GYG’s competitive advantages are quantifiable and widening in the Australian market. The risk is competitive convergence from incumbents with greater scale and marketing spend.
strengths: Proprietary clean-food supply chain (100% proprietary ingredients, no freezers in stores)
Drive-thru format economics (A$6.9M AUV, highest in QSR fast-casual)
Franchise system quality (48–53% ROI, 64% repeat rate, 108-site pipeline)
Youth demographic capture (27.7% of transactions from 18–24 age group vs 16% industry)
vulnerabilities: McDonald’s overtook GYG on same-store sales in Q4 2025: first competitive reversal since listing
Delivery platforms represent 26.6% of network sales at undisclosed (likely low) margins
US operations: −57.5% corporate restaurant margins, A$13.2M EBITDA loss in FY25
Supply chain concentration: top 5 suppliers represent 60% of purchases
competitivePositioning.keyInsight: The domestic competitive position is widening on all measurable metrics (AUV, margins, franchise ROI). The risk is not current performance but trajectory: McDonald’s overtaking on comps in Q4 2025 is the first datapoint suggesting competitive convergence. Delivery margin opacity (26.6% of sales at unknown economics) is the largest structural blind spot.

governance.title: Accountability mechanisms are nominal rather than structural
governance.overview: GYG’s governance framework creates the conditions for value-destructive capital allocation to persist. The domestic franchise operations demonstrate operational competence; the US expansion demonstrates that competence does not extend to disciplined capital allocation when accountability mechanisms are absent.

rows: Share-based compensation: 62% of FY25 statutory NP
US expansion: no defined exit criteria

governance.severityAssessment: Governance weaknesses are concentrated in capital allocation accountability rather than operational oversight. The domestic franchise system operates effectively despite governance gaps, suggesting operational competence is embedded in the management team rather than the governance framework. The critical risk is the US expansion, where A$13.2M of annual capital destruction continues without binding exit criteria, and the 44% insider control bloc prevents external accountability. Severity: MEDIUM-HIGH. The governance structure does not currently impair domestic operations but actively enables value-destructive international expansion.
governance.keyInsight: The 44.3% aligned control bloc (TDM + Marks + Barrenjoey + Russo) makes external accountability mechanisms ineffective. Board independence at 43% is below the ASX Corporate Governance Principles best practice of majority independence. The Remuneration and Nomination Committee has 2 of 3 members who are non-independent. LTI vests on time alone with zero performance conditions. This structure allows the US expansion to continue without binding milestones or exit criteria.

accountingQuality.title: Medium severity risk concentrated in the US restaurant impairment assessment
accountingQuality.overview: GYG’s accounting quality exhibits a split personality. Australian operations produce high-quality, cash-backed earnings with favourable forensic indicators. US operations contain a material impairment exposure that auditors have not flagged as a Key Audit Matter.

rows: Strong cash backing of reported earnings
First positive FCF half; inflection genuine but fragile
Unqualified opinion; US impairment not flagged as KAM

accountingQuality.usImpairmentAnalysis: The parent entity wrote off its entire A$28.2M US investment to zero. The consolidated entity concluded no impairment was required despite corporate restaurant margins of −57.5%. Cumulative unrecognised US tax losses total A$29.2M and are growing. Deloitte issued unqualified opinions across all periods; US impairment was not flagged as a Key Audit Matter. The gap between parent entity treatment (full write-off) and consolidated treatment (no impairment) creates material disclosure risk. If impairment is eventually recognised, the charge (A$10–20M) would represent 70–140% of FY25 net profit.
accountingQuality.keyInsight: Australian earnings quality is high: cash-backed (OCF/NI 3.96×), low accruals (Sloan −0.05), low manipulation risk (Beneish −2.55). The risk is concentrated entirely in the US impairment assessment, where the parent entity’s own treatment (full write-off to zero) contradicts the consolidated entity’s treatment (no impairment required). This asymmetry suggests future impairment recognition is probable.

forwardGuidance.title: Above-average credibility on Australian operations; systematic avoidance on US milestones
forwardGuidance.overview: GYG’s forward guidance exhibits a clear bifurcation. Australian operational guidance has been reliable across multiple periods. US guidance is systematically vague, with no quantified milestones that would trigger strategic review or exit.

rows: FY24: guided 26, delivered. FY25: guided 30, delivered 31

forwardGuidance.credibilityAssessment: Australian guidance is above-average credibility. Management has met or exceeded restaurant opening targets in every reported period. Margin and G&A targets are consistent with observable trends. US guidance is the weak point: the 15-restaurant cap is management-imposed and non-binding, the "proof of concept" label is used without quantified success metrics, and there are no defined exit criteria that would trigger strategic review. The absence of US milestones creates open-ended capital destruction risk under the existing governance structure.
forwardGuidance.keyInsight: Trust Australian operational guidance; discount US strategic commitments entirely. The credibility gap mirrors the governance gap: strong operational execution in a competitive domestic market, combined with undisciplined capital allocation in a market where the company has no competitive advantage.

financialHistory.title: Five-year financial trajectory: growth decelerating, margins inflecting, cash flow turning
financialHistory.overview: GYG’s financial history shows a company transitioning from hyper-growth to mature growth, with the cash flow inflection point occurring in FY25. The central question is whether the FY25 inflection represents a new normal (sustainable positive cash flow) or a temporary pause in the growth investment cycle.
financialHistory.cashQuality: OCF/NI of 3.96×, Sloan accrual ratio of −0.05, Beneish M-Score of −2.55. H2 FY25 produced the first positive FCF half (A$1.1M). Cash: A$281.7M, zero interest-bearing debt. Earnings quality is high; earnings quantity is the constraint.
financialHistory.keyInsight: Revenue has compounded from A$131M (FY21) to A$468M (FY25), but the growth rate has decelerated in every period (41% → 50% → 32% → 28%). Gross margins are remarkably stable (74.5–76.0%), suggesting strong pricing power. The EBIT margin trough in FY24 (−1.1%) reflected IPO costs and US losses; FY25 recovery to 5.4% is genuine but fragile at 4–5% comparable sales growth. OCF inflected from −A$9.7M (FY24) to +A$57.3M (FY25), the most important single datapoint in the financial history.

beliefTrajectory.title: Extreme variance to post-IPO valuation distribution
beliefTrajectory.overview: GYG’s market narrative has undergone a complete reversal in 20 months. The stock listed as a "generational compounder" at 90× EBITDA and now trades at 23×, below its IPO price. The belief trajectory tracks the transition from growth premium to mature operator discount.

milestones.event: IPO listing at A$22.00. Oversubscribed. Priced as generational Australian compounder.
All-time high. 90.4× trailing EV/EBITDA. Peak scarcity premium.
First negative filing-day reaction (−9.2%). Death cross. Institutional selling begins.
First escrow release (25% of pre-IPO shares). Selling intensifies.
Second escrow release (75%). TDM sell-down visible (36.1% to 20.6%). Second negative filing-day (−13.9%).
Break below IPO price. Retail capitulation. Psychological support lost.
New all-time low. Third consecutive negative filing-day (−17.6%). 74% multiple compression from peak.

marketDynamics: Down-day volume: 1.87× up-day volume (capitulation indicator, not accumulation)
TDM Growth Partners reduced managed stake: 36.1% → 20.6% (mandate transfers, August 2025)
EBITDA doubled (A$33M → A$69M) yet multiple compressed 74%: fundamental improvement offset by re-rating
beliefTrajectory.keyInsight: The belief trajectory reveals a market that has completely reversed its assessment of GYG in 20 months. The IPO narrative of a generational compounder is being replaced by a mature franchise operator narrative. The most telling datapoint: EBITDA doubled from A$33M to A$69M while the EV/EBITDA multiple compressed from 90.4× to 23.1×. The stock is cheaper on fundamentals than at IPO despite being a larger, more profitable business. This either represents an overcorrection (N1) or correct repricing (N2). The H1 FY26 results will determine which interpretation is correct.

scenarioAnalysis.title: Probability-weighted scenario analysis with three-year earnings paths
scenarioAnalysis.overview: Four competing scenarios with explicit earnings projections and valuation assumptions. Each scenario is assigned a probability weight based on the evidence assessment. The evidence-weighted price aggregates across all scenarios.

scenarios.title: Australian Compounder Temporarily Mispriced
scenarios.confirmIf: Comparable sales reach 6%+ in any H2 FY26 quarter with a positive filing-day reaction
scenarios.ruleOutIf: Comparable sales fall below 3% or EV/EBITDA multiple compresses below 15×

scenarios.title: Decelerating Growth Operator (Modal Outcome)
scenarios.confirmIf: Comparable sales fall below 3% in any quarter, confirming pure inflation pass-through
scenarios.ruleOutIf: Comparable sales re-accelerate to 8%+ for two consecutive quarters

scenarios.title: US Capital Destruction Accelerates
scenarios.confirmIf: US EBITDA loss exceeds A$8M in H1 FY26 (annualising above A$16M)
scenarios.ruleOutIf: US restaurant-level margins turn positive in any quarter

scenarios.title: Franchise System Stress
scenarios.confirmIf: Franchisees on royalty relief exceed 10, or median franchise ROI falls below 40%
scenarios.ruleOutIf: Franchise ROI stabilises above 48% and royalty relief count falls to 4 or below

ewpCalculation.robustness: Directional (survives perturbation tests) but not magnitude-robust at ±10% threshold. Small shifts in N1/N2 weights (±5pp) change the EWP by A$0.70–1.20 but do not change the directional conclusion (overpriced).
